ORIGINALITY.justification: The work primarily applies and integrates existing tools (Wasserstein/EMD for documents from Kusner et al. 2015, D2-clustering from Li & Wang 2008, and modified B-ADMM from Ye et al. 2017) to make EMD-based document clustering scalable (Sec. 2–3 and 3.3). The paper’s novelty lies in the practical combination and engineering (K‑means‑style D2‑clustering with Elkan pruning and parallel B‑ADMM) rather than a fundamentally new theory or algorithm; it is a useful, moderate extension that systematizes applying recent barycenter solvers to document analysis. The authors clearly differentiate from Kusner et al. (2015) by emphasizing a clustering framework and scalability (Sec. 2), but core techniques are existing methods adapted to this application.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important practical bottleneck—making Wasserstein/EMD-based document comparison and D2-clustering scalable—by adopting a modified B-ADMM solver and parallel implementations (sec. 3.3, Ye et al. 2017) and pruning via Elkan’s idea (sec. 4.3). Experiments show consistent gains over common baselines (Tf-idf, LDA, NMF, PV) across multiple datasets and report realistic runtimes and large reductions in pairwise distance computations compared to Kusner et al. (2015), indicating the approach could change practitioners’ toolset for document clustering. Because it renders nonparametric, embedding-aware clustering practical and provides code/datasets, it is likely to be built upon broadly, though its impact is primarily in clustering/document analysis rather than truly transformative across all NLP.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates on six diverse datasets (short/long/domain-specific) and reports multiple clustering metrics (Homogeneity/Completeness/V-measure, AMI, ARI), runtime on large datasets, and ensemble/iteration statistics (Section 4.1–4.3), which supports its main claims. It includes sensitivity analysis for hyperparameters K and centroid size m (2D plots, multiple m values) and reports practical speedups (Elkan pruning), but lacks ablations isolating the impact of key algorithmic choices (e.g., modified B-ADMM vs alternatives) and detailed preprocessing/embedding hyperparameters for all baselines. Related work on Wasserstein/EMD and Kusner et al. is discussed fairly, and limitations (runtime, Ohsumed domain-embedding needs) are acknowledged; reproducibility is aided by stated code/data release but some experimental/configuration specifics are missing.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper studies applying efficient Wasserstein-barycenter/D2-clustering over word-embedding document representations (Sec. 3.1–3.3) and focuses on scalability and algorithmic improvements (modified B-ADMM) which are central concerns for both ML and NLP venues. It evaluates on standard NLP datasets (20Newsgroups, Reuters, BBC, Ohsumed) with accepted clustering metrics (Homogeneity, AMI, ARI) and compares to relevant baselines (LDA, NMF, PV, Tf-idf, Kusner et al. 2015), matching community expectations (Sec. 4.1–4.3). Given the topical relevance (optimal transport + word embeddings + document clustering), audience, and standard experimental setup, this is an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally organized in a logical order (related work -> method -> experiments -> discussion) and the main algorithmic steps are laid out (e.g. Sections 3.1–3.3 and Eq. (5)–(13)). However, the excerpt contains many duplicated sentences (e.g. multiple repeated "Thanks to the recent advance..." paragraphs), formatting/artifact issues in equations and notation (odd symbols such as ⇧, ⇡, and unclear subscripts/superscripts in Eq. (2) and the B-ADMM updates), and several references to figures/tables (Fig. 1, Table 1, Table 3) that are not present here. These presentation problems and minor notational inconsistencies make parts of the methods and runtime discussion harder to follow and would benefit from careful editing and cleanup.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear claims (Wasserstein/EMD-based D2‑clustering using modified B‑ADMM yields better clustering and practical runtimes) and presents experiments on six datasets with standard metrics and runtimes (Sections 4.1, 4.3). However the mathematical presentation of the modified B‑ADMM updates (Eqs. (5)–(13)) contains ambiguous/likely-typoed expressions (normalization indices, missing difference in the dual update) so derivations are not fully verifiable from the text. The experimental methodology is mostly sound (appropriate baselines, multiple datasets, 50 runs ensemble) but lacks reported statistical tests and full numeric results in the provided excerpt to fully support the strong universal superiority claims. Overall the technical approach is plausible but some derivations and some supporting experimental detail/claims are insufficiently substantiated in the provided text.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of appropriate baselines (Tf-idf, AvgDoc, LDA, NMF, PV, spectral/LSI/LPP, and the Kusner et al. WMD-based method) and uses multiple standard datasets and metrics (Homogeneity/Completeness/V-measure, AMI, ARI) as described in Sections 4.1 and 5. It also reports runtime and scalability comparisons versus Kusner et al. and discusses methodological differences (Sec. 2 and 4.3). The authors acknowledge when a baseline (Tfidf-N on BBCSport) performs best and analyze sensitivity to hyperparameters (Sec. 5). Minor omissions include limited detail on hyperparameter tuning and exact fairness of runtime environments for all baselines, but overall comparisons are comprehensive and relevant.
